We will not tolerate that if you walk out of here today. When you're gone, play offense, not defense, to continue manipulating it. It's as simple as that, but again, I'll put out technical training on how to manipulate it in the near future. Make sense? Okay, very good.
Okay, A players, unreal the level of result that we've seen from people who hire the right people per usual. Making people a lot more money. I'll tell you, I get on consulting deals occasionally. G's a month to do a consulting deal with them and they'll be all excited. They're like, 'I'm gonna pay you know, talk to my ad guys, like, let me know if they're shit. I'm like, just get on the call. You'll see it live.' The amount of people you tolerate that just suck. It's unreal to me. Unbelievable to me. You pay too little, you expect too much, and yet you absolutely suck at developing people into great people. That's the illusion you have. You think I can pay people less, develop into being better.
And I'll get away with paying them less because I'll make them great. But then you never actually make them great because you suck at developing people into being great. What you should actually do instead is fucking nut up, spend more money on payroll, and get better people into the organization that are probably to actually move the needle that you're desperately attempting to hire them to move. That always works with a higher probability compared to tolerating donkeys. Does that make sense? Some of you may not remember where that originated, and I'll tell you the story. 2018 or 2017, I don't remember, we had the Internet Earners Summit, which was an event we used to throw. We'd get a big house in the hills out in L. A.
We'd have seven, eight, nine-figure earners get up who are making internet money and talk to us. One of them was named Anthony Lawley. He was one of the 100 million guys. He's a real estate guy. And he starts talking about staff, and he described them in three ways. Stallions, camels, and donkeys. He said, 'Stallions, very powerful creatures. Stallions are the little Ferrari logo. An actual stallion in real life.' Will make you think that that horse could kill you from how powerful it is. It is unreal the level of power that a true stallion, a real-life stallion, holds. Stallions, as employees or contractors, just need direction. They just mean told. Go this way. This is the outcome. And they'll do it. You need to then get the fuck out of their way.
Or you might get hurt. Camels, very reliable. You can take a camel across the desert and need it to drink water once. But does a camel, have you ever seen a camel walking around without a person leading it somewhere? What do camels do when they're not being led? You've seen videos of camels. What do they do? They don't do fucking shit. They just sit there. They lay down. But when you lead a camel, it goes long distances. But it needs lead. To go the distance. Donkeys. You've probably never seen a video of a productive donkey. Because you've got to beat a donkey to get it to do anything. You literally have to beat the donkey. It wore, yee-haw, yee-haw. Won't do a fucking thing. Just sit there, all unproductive.
While it consumes your resources. Donkeys still need to eat. They're not immortal. Yeah, right. I'm sure very few of you, if any of you, have met somebody who's owned a donkey before. They hate it. There's nothing worse than owning a donkey, and you can't give it away because nobody wants it. You don't want donkeys. But all of you likely have a donkey in your organization, maybe even a few. And you tolerate them because they have, again, donkeys still have some base level of productivity. But it's exhausting to get it to produce because you've got to beat the donkey to get it to do anything. You ideally have a mix of stallions and camels. That's the proper combo, Anthony said to us at that event. And it forever reshaped my my view.
Never really in these most recent years have to manage you or do anything that you would consider me managing you. How much money did you make last month? Leo makes $70,000 a month now. Give it up for Leo. He died in the letter. Leo's a stallion. He doesn't look very scary though, right? Just looks like a normal dude. Got his daughter there. Leo's got four kids. Beautiful family. I don't have to tell Leo anything other than... This is the outcome, big dog. Now he's on his way. I got plenty of camels though. Marcos, where you at? You're not a camel. Lutz is a camel. Lutz hits me up every week. I got to give them the same feedback every week on those damn tweets. But he's reliable.
And he gets it done. I don't have to beat him like a donkey. But I've got to lead him like a camel. And that's okay, because I'm aware that he's a camel. And so, therefore, I act accordingly. If I tried to treat Leo like a camel, that would piss him the fuck off. That would lower his quality of life. If I tried to treat Lutz and Marco's team as a stallion, my tweets would look all kinds of fucked up. You understand? If I lack the self-awareness of how to behave with a specific staff member relative to how they are, I'm going to have a bad result and a bad outcome. And God forbid I have a fucking donkey. I'm going to have a bad time. Does that make sense? Now back to that whole certainty thing. You know what happens when you kill a donkey? You'll barely fucking notice.
Back to that spectrum. Right? Don't be a pussy and kill the donkey. And treat your camels like camels and treat your stallions like stallions. Low ticket to high ticket is still absolutely ripping. Dramatically increase the quality of calls and the consistency of calls that come from DMNs. Okay, since half the room's watched it and half the room hasn't watched it, this is what I would encourage you to do as an action item.
Go log into the training library. And watch the video in the weekly group calls library. On the DM ad lessons. We tested this specifically. DM ads historically have about... 100K to 300K a month cap. And usually they go into the shitter after that. When you optimize around scheduled calls comparatively to optimizing around the DM ad itself, we've seen this funnel go all the way up to about 600K a month now, which is very impressive considering it's double what it's previously been limited to. Okay, so long story short, there's also been a few things that have really fucked people. And we're going to go through those two. Number one. I have seen this more in this last quarter than I ever have before. It's like a parasite exists within your company and it's your sales team. Who here has heard of a tapeworm? Raise your hand. Now, tapeworms can kill people.
Have you ever heard of a tapeworm killing somebody? It does. What have you also heard that tapeworms can do? They can cause weight loss. Some people intentionally consume a tapeworm to try to lose weight. Why do they confidently do that? Because they can manage the parasite. The people who die from tapeworms. Maybe they're aware that it's in their system. But some people aren't even aware they have a tapeworm. And it kills them. Because they're not in control of the parasite within. I've had to say in the last two weeks that two different sales teams were terrorist organizations. I compared sales teams to a cartel within Mexico. The government knows the cartel exists. Yet they tolerate the cartel because the cartel's got too much power over them.
And they deal with this back and forth relationship that we all witness from the outside. How does it get so bad is the question. It's the lack of the iron fist. You have to control it or it gets way out of hand. We had clients say, 'And to be fair, this is very important to understand this next part, okay?' Because I'm not trying to demonize sales teams. Sales teams are awesome. The sales team's incentive. It's typically gross commission. Right? Who here doesn't pay their salespeople on gross commission? So you all do. Gross commission. Does not give a fuck about net profit, does it? What would the sales team logically want? What would they need for them to make more money? More calls. More opportunities.
So what is the sales team always, no matter what, going to press you to do? We need more calls, man. We need more calls. Spend more money. We need more calls, dude. I'm going to have to fire people. People are going to quit if we don't get these damn calls. You're a human being. You have a conscious. Most of you. And you feel bad because you're financially responsible for these people that have taken a risk on working for you. Go ahead, man. Scale the spend. Get the more calls going. We had a client spend $250,000 on a call funnel in the month of August. They lost $111 per call. Every call that was booked, they lost $111 per call. The sales team, every single fucking day.
We need more calls. People are going to quit. The client... after they looked at these results in September said, 'Let them quit. Fuck you!' scale that spin straight back down. Cost per call reduced. Two-thirds. We had a $300 to $400 cost per call, reducing the spend, pulled it back down to $150. Profitability that funnel went right back up to where it should be. And a few people had to quit. Is that good or bad for the business? Huh? The parasite within. Makes sense? You manage it or it fucks you and can kill you. There are natural ebbs and flows in a business where you will scale. You will make more money. You may reduce profitability as a result of scaling. To attempt, you push it open.
Wow. Hey, Will, let's take a note that we need to put a little sign on the door that says 'push.' And then on the inside so he doesn't get trapped in there. Hold. I didn't think we'd need to do that. Whatever.
Back to my point, okay? I digress. I can't stress this enough. You will face uncomfortable decisions that will force you to have to let people go. Let them quit. When you don't have the opportunities for them to pursue, and that's okay. You will likely not perpetually scale. Now, I'm going to be very fair in saying, I actually want to give a big shout-out to Josh and his team, Wires from Strangers, because Josh is one of the only people, as a sales manager that I've seen, that will— not over hire on reps. He's actually very, very mindful of dealing with sales teams and marketers. The marketers will typically be very confident when things are good on the capacity to add more call volume and maintain costs.
Those will have ebbs and flows as well. And when the confidence is there, the marketers, like me, will be screaming higher, higher, higher. And then naturally they'll hire. And as a result of that, we'll book in a lot of calls, experience that rut that occurs, and that's when the people have to get fired and or quit when there's not enough calls and a little bit of pullback has to occur. We generally don't have to deal with that when we work with Josh. Because his team will push against me at all costs, responsibly. on. Preventing the over hiring from occurring, let me be super clear when I say this. Have you had an expectation that was set with your sales team? That you tell them, 'Look, right now things are really good.
I can book in a fuckload of calls for you right now.' But there may be a period of time where I don't get to do that. And then you may want to quit. I just want to make that aware up front. Would you feel a lot better come the time that they need to quit? If you set the expectation, yes or no, would you feel better come that time? When you don't set the expectation, how does it feel comparatively? That's when the emotion takes over. All right, to be clear, another thing that's really fucked people are really high CPM costs. This feels very random, and that's why I put that word. Feels chaotic. You'll be dealing with like $60 CPMs one day, the next day they're like $300.
So far, there's a pretty random set of solutions, too. It could be following the Andromeda best practices. It could relate to the fact that learning phase in the ads manager is having a lot of impact right now negatively. When you first launch a campaign, sometimes it can look like complete Dog shit on a hot day. It's already been out there for a few days. And then it'll just all come back down to normal. Sometimes, though, if you wait, it won't come back down to normal and it may actually get worse. We find that relaunching the campaign a few times can actually help mitigate this more than almost anything else that we've found is a solution so far. But if you're dealing with this, be sure to... reach out.
We have a whole list of what we go through to help resolve this, and I will say you really got to rotate through several things from what we've seen to get this to crack back down if you unfortunately get plagued with this. Okay, to be clear, the conversion APIs I mentioned working really well. We're seeing, I think in real time, the failure of browser events. So when you put a pixel on your page, the pixel reporting back the data instead of sending back data manually through the conversion API. We're seeing, I think, in real time, a massive transition away from the standard pixel reporting that we've all historically relied upon.
For some reason, seeing higher match rates through the conversion API beyond just browser pixel blockers, the conversion API is really helping people dial in. The right types of people, hitting the pixel. More than ever before, this last quarter, I've had to tell so many of you to link your conversion API to a manual trigger in your CRM. So letting your salespeople, as an example, mark somebody as qualified and then sending that data back instead. I've had to tell more of you that than ever before. So we're seeing that really fuck people too. We practice the art of detachment around here. How many of you like watches or cars? Raise your hand. Have you held on to a watch or a car for too long? There's an ancient story. about a monkey that wanted a nut. and the nut was in a tree stump. And the monkey reaches his hand in the stump. Grabs the nut. But he can't get his hand out of the stump.
His fist made his hand too big. And the stump holds. was too small. All he had to do— We let go of that damn nut. Obviously, he doesn't get to walk away with the nut if he lets go, though. But he would get to escape with his damn monkey, stubborn as can be. Holding on to that damn nut, just trying to yank its arm out. A tiger comes up. And tigers. Love to eat monkeys. But it's a rare treat because usually they can get away. This tiger comes up and the monkey sees the tiger. He knows death is inevitable. Really wants that nut though. Wants that nut so bad. Willing to die over him. All the monkey has to do is let go. Temporary loss. For arguably. Survival.
And who knows what else on the other side of survival, right? I ask you a simple question. Are you currently attached to something that makes no sense to continue doing, but you're afraid to stop doing it? Because there's no certainty on the other side of new actions. There's at least a little bit of certainty on the other side of the current action. The monkey knows it's got a nut already. He's holding on to it. Are there no more nuts in the world? Are there no more nuts in the jungle for the monkey to get? How fucking stupid does the monkey sound? For not letting go. And thinking there's no more nuts. Are you the monkey? That's going to hold on to that nut. Or are you going to let it go?
This has fucked a tremendous amount of you over this last quarter. There's been a lot to detach from. Simply put, change is one of the only constants. New audiences equal new messaging adaptation. We've seen... through this Andromeda update, little pocket audiences that you're reaching that you've never historically reached. And what we have to do is very interesting. If we see an ad that distributes the most, we then go look at the messaging of that ad and we align the landing page to it. Because I can launch 20 to 30 creatives, but one to three will get all the reach. Wouldn't it make sense to update the page or center it to the messaging that the one or three are talking about? Wouldn't we want a page that aligns?
To whoever our ads are reaching at the highest rate. We have to do new things like that. We've never really had to do that in the past besides Google AdWords campaigns. You did the most easy thing to do. You talked to ChatGPT with your specific tech stack. And you solve the problem how fast? After you finally just talked to ChatGPT. So how a weak... of a problem being tolerated that was solved in five minutes. Could you have gotten to the point where you asked Chad TPT faster within that week? Now, I ask you this. Every problem you face from here forward, you remember that timeline of how fucked you made yourself on the speed in which you were able to overcome it.
All because you couldn't get to the point where you just did something like talking to ChatGPT faster. We've got to rotate how many things we try in a rapid succession until we get the outcome. Does that make sense to you? It's that easy. The tolerance— of not rotating through solutions faster— is what fucks you. Thank you. Does that make sense? Grow faster by solving problems faster and understand there is never going to be a point where you no longer have a problem. You will have a problem until you literally die. You will have problems going into the grave, which likely include the dying part. You understand? You'll never reach a period of time where it's fucking lollipops and rainbows and your business is just flying up.
And you're just so like, 'Oh my God, this is fucking awesome.' I don't have anything to do besides just make a fuckload of money. I got everything I've ever wanted and don't have any kind of issues now that I own it all. You know, I had a guy. He owns. A big house. 52,000 square feet. And a monster, monster crib. I don't want that. You know why? This motherfucker spends 70K a month on upkeep on that piece. That's payroll for his house. That's the cost to own it. Some of you dream of owning shit and you forget that it costs money to own it. You get your dream car and you forget. I'm going to have to take it to the mechanic occasionally. bought three Richard Mills this month.
One of them had an issue. I was all deflated. Oh, my new watch, man. I've got to go take it to get service. You know why it was deflated? Because I forgot that there's a cost to own it. I forgot that there are a bunch more problems I'm going to experience even after I accomplish what I wanted to accomplish. Do not let that escape your mind. Or you will become soft. And slow. You understand? The aggressive action that you must take is backed by the certainty that there's another problem on the other side of it. And we have to be enthusiastic about solving that fucking thing too. Because guess what? Our life gets better as we do. That's the good news. Solving problems doesn't make our life worse. It makes our life better. And that's why we do it. That's why we endure on that journey. You understand? Very good. Now give yourselves a big round of applause. Congratulations.
